data.title: Ceasefire Without An Endgame: Tech Climbs The Wall Of Hormuz
data.description:
We’re back on a new Monday, albeit with the same old ceasefire in effect. Last week’s deadline for the U.S. and Iran to find common ground passed with no resolution. The deadlock we described a week ago in the “No Pressure Ceasefire” largely remains unchanged, with the U.S. and Iran not making it to another round of talks in Islamabad. While Trump had emphasized that last week’s deadline would see no extensions, we ultimately saw one emerge, with the explanation that it would give Iran’s “fractured” leadership time. Trump noted that:

Based on the fact that the Government of Iran is seriously fractured ... we have been asked to hold our Attack on the Country of Iran until such time as their leaders and representatives can come up with a unified proposal.

This time around, however, there is no deadline at all. Instead, the ceasefire is expected to last “until such time as their proposal is submitted and discussions are concluded, one way or the other.” Trump is holding talks with top security advisors this Monday on the Iran situation to move negotiations forward after Iran submitted a new deal, which Washington is rumored not to be too fond of. The Strait of Hormuz, which has been the most painful form of this war's global impact, continues to be closed by both sides: Iran has blocked the Strait, and the U.S. naval blockade remains imposed. Neither appears to be in any rush to reopen it before, at least, the other side retreats or a resolution is reached. Trump noted that:

When you have vast amounts of oil pouring through your system ... if for any reason this line is closed because you can't put it into containers or ships ... what happens is that the line explodes from within. ... They say they only have about three days before that happens.

So, we’re still in a ceasefire with a soaring market, led by tech, a closed Strait, and no clarity on the deadline of this now almost 60-day-old war.

An interesting phenomenon that surfaced over the last week amid all this is semis gaining huge momentum, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) crossing $10,000 for the first time ever (in fact, ending the prior week at around $9,555 and between two Fridays rising to $10,550), while software wanes for the most part, or at least in comparison. Since Q2 began, the outperforming league within our universe has been dominated by the semi industry. Last Thursday alone, semis outperformed software by an estimated 8.4%, and since April of last year, “the ratio of semiconductor stocks relative to software stocks has fallen -70 points, to the lowest in history.”

Movement on the Chip Wars?

Just within the past 24 hours, China’s National Development and Reform Commission blocked Meta Platforms, Inc.'s (META) acquisition of AI start-up Manus, noting that it will “prohibit foreign investment in Manus in accordance with laws and regulations and require the parties involved to withdraw the acquisition transaction.” This comes after China’s commerce ministry announced plans to investigate the acquisition at the start of the year. The NDRC's office is not acting in a vacuum; it is a visible response to the back-and-forth chip wars between the U.S. and China, in our opinion. While headlines are consumed with the louder U.S.-Iran war playing out, the U.S.-China chip wars are far from on pause. The House Foreign Affairs Committee, just a couple of days ago, cleared 20 new export control measures to tighten the chokehold on China’s access to advanced tech. Earlier this month, the U.S. introduced the MATCH Act, a draft bill that targets DUV tools sold by ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) and advanced etch tools sold by others. ASML took a steep hit on the news of the MATCH Act. We’re just recapping the past couple of weeks, but these fall into a longer tug-of-war between the U.S. and China in the tech race.

We’re circling back to Chip Wars now because a much-anticipated and pushed-out summit is approaching between Trump and China’s Xi in Beijing in mid-May. The move from the NDRC is telling of deeper internal politics and each nation’s fight to hold the global AI lead without giving the other any freebies. The background to Manus’ movements over the past year is also noteworthy at this juncture, because after raising $75M from Benchmark back in May of last year, Manus closed its China operations in July and moved to Singapore, where it could avoid the overhang of China's regulatory approval process. The staff has since even moved into Meta’s own offices in Singapore. The way we’re looking at the situation, this appears to be a warning of sorts from Chinese authorities to China’s AI start-up community, because what Manus and Meta may have pulled off sets a dangerous precedent that others could follow. Manus has been gaining more traction lately, as shown below, and as the AI race heats up, we believe China wants to safeguard its talent.

We’ve been waiting for the U.S.-China Summit since it was pushed from late March to early April and then to May. This meeting matters not only because it may ease the current geopolitical tensions around the Strait of Hormuz amid the ongoing ceasefire deadlock but also because it may be telling for CEO Jensen Huang’s efforts to bring Nvidia back to China.

The Analog Boom

In late March, we published a newsletter titled “Another Monday Madness: A Tech Take,” and within it, we pitched our answer to Where To Hide Out In Tech, and the answer was analog. We explained that:

When there are panics over price pressure, shortages, and longer lead times, everyone and their grandmother will pull in orders, or even double orders, to secure capacity at a more favorable price. Now, this happens across the supply chain, but the reason analog is special is that, unlike other sectors, analog tech doesn’t easily go out of fashion. Remember, we’re not talking about 5nm moving to 3nm; we’re talking about old generations. So, the backlash for holding more inventory than ideal on these chips if end demand doesn’t kick in is more manageable.

We saw this happen last April in the aftermath of Trump’s Liberation Day tariffs, and we strongly expected to see it happen again in reaction to rising supply chain risk as the Strait remains closed for longer. Likewise, we cited Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) as our top pick for its diversified exposure across several end markets, which would bode well for our double-ordering thesis. We got the confirmation that we’re on the right track after TXN reported its Q1 '26 results after the bell last Wednesday, and the stock surged as much as 19% the day after. Management beat for Q1, as sales rose 9% Q/Q to $4.83B versus estimates of $4.53B. Here’s the breakdown of sales: analog was up 9% Q/Q to $3.92B, while embedded grew 9% sequentially as well to $723M. Industrial sales were up 20% Q/Q; auto sales were flat Q/Q; personal electronics were up 2% Q/Q; data center sales were up 25% Q/Q; and communications were up by about 30% Q/Q.

Outlook was the part we expected to be the booster, and management didn’t disappoint, guiding comfortably ahead of consensus for $5B to $5.40B against a consensus of $4.87B. The Q2 outlook is above typical seasonality, which management attributes to strength in the industrial and data center sectors but which we critically view as the result of pulls and double ordering. We expect this momentum to benefit TXN for one more quarter before letting up. TXN serves as a leading indicator for the analog peer group because it reports before the bunch, and we expect to see the momentum spill into others, including ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON) and Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI). We expect our analog thesis to hold for the rest of the peer group during this earnings season, particularly as the Strait remains closed, meaning supply chain anxieties have yet to subside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
